name: BPC-157 (Body Protection Compound-157)
summary: BPC-157 is a stable pentadecapeptide (15 amino acids) derived from a protective protein found in human gastric juice. It is studied for accelerating healing of tendons, ligaments, muscle, bone, and gut tissue, with strong anti-inflammatory and angiogenic effects in animal models.
mechanism:
BPC-157 is a partial sequence of body protection compound, a peptide isolated from human gastric juice, and is notably stable in gastric acid. It promotes tendon fibroblast outgrowth, cell survival under stress, and cell migration, effects linked to activation of the FAK-paxillin signaling pathway. It increases collagen formation and supports fibroblast activity, and dose-dependently boosts in vitro migration of tendon fibroblasts.

A central mechanism is the promotion of angiogenesis, partly through upregulation of VEGF and modulation of the nitric oxide (NO) system, improving blood supply to injured tissue. BPC-157 also enhances growth hormone receptor expression in tendon fibroblasts, reduces inflammatory cytokines, and exerts cytoprotective effects across the GI tract, blood vessels, and nervous system. Together these actions accelerate healing of wounds, tendons, ligaments, muscle, and bone in preclinical models.
benefits: Accelerates healing of tendon, ligament, muscle, and bone injuries (animal models)
Promotes angiogenesis and improves blood flow to damaged tissue
Gut-protective; studied as an adjunct for GI and IBD-related healing
Enhances collagen formation and fibroblast activity
Upregulates growth hormone receptor expression in tendon fibroblasts
risks: Not FDA-approved; no human clinical safety or efficacy data established
Banned by WADA and prohibited in professional sport
Most evidence is from rodent studies; human dosing and effects are extrapolated
Long-term safety and angiogenic effects in humans are unknown
sideEffects: Mild nausea or GI upset (more common with oral dosing)

dosage.notes: Common educational protocol is 250-500 mcg per day; e.g. 250 mcg twice daily = 500 mcg/day to maintain stable levels. Subcutaneous injection near the injury site is often used for localized effect; oral forms also exist for gut-focused use.
dosage.timing: Typically dosed once or twice daily, with no strict food requirement (can be taken with or without food). Subcutaneous injections are commonly given systemically or near the injury site for a localized effect. Unlike titrated peptides, BPC-157 is generally held at a fixed daily level rather than ramped up.
dosage.maxDose: Community use rarely exceeds ~500-1000 mcg/day. This ceiling is anecdotal and not clinically established; no human trials have defined a safe maximum, and higher doses have not been shown to improve outcomes.

steps.note: 250 mcg once or twice daily (250-500 mcg/day). Human protocols are anecdotal and not clinically validated; derived from community use and extrapolated from rodent ng/kg-mcg/kg studies.
Optional 1-2 week extension to align with the injury-recovery window; taper off or stop once healing goals are met.

halfLife: Short; metabolized in the liver with a half-life under ~30 minutes, cleared by the kidneys
cycleLength: Typically 4-8 weeks per cycle, often run alongside the injury-recovery period

reconstitution.notes: 5 mg vial + 2 mL bacteriostatic water = 2.5 mg/mL (2500 mcg/mL), so 250 mcg = 0.1 mL (10 units on a U-100 insulin syringe). Swirl gently to dissolve.

storage: Store lyophilized vials frozen or refrigerated. After reconstitution, refrigerate at 2-8C and use within ~4 weeks. Protect from light.
contraindications: Active or history of cancer (caution due to angiogenic activity)
Competitive athletes subject to anti-doping testing

interactions.note: Core of the 'Wolverine stack.' BPC-157 builds repair infrastructure (angiogenesis, growth factors, collagen) while TB-500 mobilizes and migrates repair cells into position; the two target overlapping but distinct healing pathways for enhanced tissue repair.

references.title: The promoting effect of pentadecapeptide BPC 157 on tendon healing (PubMed)
